serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw banana flower is approximately 100 g (about 1 cup chopped), commonly used as a salad base or prepared vegetable in Southeast and South Asian cuisines.

nutrient_source.title: Banana flower, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for banana flower, raw (cr_id F14040016). All core macros and micronutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and sugar values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (banana flower, raw) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw banana flower is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 (low) is estimated by analogy to other high-fibre, low-carbohydrate tropical vegetables documented in peer-reviewed GI literature. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (51 × 1.52) / 100 ≈ 0.8. This is a conservative analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for banana flower is not available in approved sources as of 2026.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100 g) and high potassium (447 mg/100 g) yield an outstanding Na:K ratio supportive of blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Raw banana flower is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its exceptionally low sodium content (3 mg/100 g) and high potassium (447 mg/100 g) align with DASH dietary principles, and it should be prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (1.52 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (4.3 g/100 g), and low estimated GI (51) result in minimal postprandial glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw banana flower is an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; its very low carbohydrate content, high soluble fibre, and low estimated GI (51) support stable postprandial blood glucose, provided it is not prepared with high-sugar sauces or sweetened coconut milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.09 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (4.3 g/100 g), and high potassium (447 mg/100 g) collectively support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw banana flower is cardioprotective; its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting vascular tone make it a strongly recommended vegetable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (4.3 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome and immune regulation; vitamin C (14 mg/100 g) provides antioxidant activity relevant to inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw banana flower is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its high fibre content supports gut-immune axis regulation, and its vitamin C content contributes antioxidant protection, but it should be prepared without pro-inflammatory cooking fats or high-sodium condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banana flower (raw) is the large, purple-red, teardrop-shaped inflorescence of the banana plant (Musa spp.), harvested before the inner florets mature into fruit. In its raw state it has a firm, slightly waxy texture and a mild astringent, slightly bitter taste due to tannins in the outer bracts. It is widely consumed across Southeast Asia (Thailand, Vietnam, Indonesia, Malaysia, Singapore) and South Asia (India, Sri Lanka), where it is used raw in salads, or cooked in curries, stir-fries, and soups after the tough outer bracts are removed and the inner florets are sliced and soaked in acidulated water to prevent browning. Nutritionally, raw banana flower is a low-calorie (24 kcal/100 g), low-carbohydrate (1.52 g/100 g) vegetable with an exceptionally high dietary fibre content (4.3 g/100 g), very low sodium (3 mg/100 g), and high potassium (447 mg/100 g). Its fat content is negligible (0.36 g/100 g) and it contains modest protein (1.58 g/100 g). These properties make it clinically relevant for blood glucose management (low estimated GI ~51, minimal glycaemic load), blood pressure control (outstanding Na:K ratio aligned with DASH principles), and cardiovascular health (negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high fibre). It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. The flower is also a source of vitamin C (~14 mg/100 g) and iron (0.47 mg/100 g). In Singapore and Malaysia it is known as jantung pisang; in Tamil as vaazhaipoo; in Hindi as kele ka phool.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040016) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and sugar sub-fractions adapted from USDA FoodData Central (banana flower, raw) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions; adaptation is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI literature as no direct measurement exists for banana flower in the University of Sydney GI Database. Vitamin C value (14 mg/100 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central as a corroborating approved source. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source is exact and the adaptations are minor and documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.09 g), monounsaturated fat (0.03 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g), and sugar (0.5 g) values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (banana flower, raw) as the HPB Foodo source does not report these sub-fractions. 2. Vitamin C (14 mg/100 g) is sourced from USDA FoodData Central as a corroborating approved source; HPB does not report vitamin C for this entry. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient fractions for banana flower raw are not fully reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0 (confirmed zero): banana flower is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12 or vitamin D by nature. 5. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero): banana flower is a plant food and contains no cholesterol. 6. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero): raw plant food with no industrial processing. 7. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI literature; no direct GI measurement for raw banana flower exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest defensible analogous anchor; reviewer should verify against the latest EFSA FoodEx2 release. 9. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been noted: while banana flower is very low in net carbohydrates, formal keto classification depends on total daily intake context and is not asserted as a hard flag here. 10. Input GI source listed as 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.5 — this is not an approved source; GI has been re-sourced to peer-reviewed analogous estimate as documented above.
